content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 15,
1993
William Cainil
City Attorney
City of Commerce
2535
Commerce Way
Commerce,
CA
90040
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-92—608
Dear Mr.
Camil:
This letter is in response to your request for advice under
the conflict-of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.
2
-!
Since you are seeking advice concerning an unidentified member of
your city council,
your letter is considered a request for
informal assistance. 2/
QUESTION
Can a member of the city council vote on a proposed
reorganization which will increase the salary for his son—in—law
who is also a source of income to the councilmexnber?
CONCLUSION
The city councilmember is prohibited from participating in
the proposed reorganization plan if the decision will have a
material financial effect on the son-in-law.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329 Cc) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
1—92—608
Page Two
FACTS
3
’
The city council for the City of Commerce will be considering
a proposed reorganization of departments within the city.
One of
the city councilmember’s son—in—law is the head of one of the
departments.
Part of the reorganization plan includes the
reassignment of duties and an increase in salary for the son—in—
law.
The son-in-law resides in a building owned by the
councilmember.
You indicated that the son—in—law pays fair market
rent to the councilmember.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
A public official includes every
natural person who is a member, officer,
employee,
or consultant
of a local or state government agency.
Members of the city
council are public officials.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
This letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)


Advice File No.
1-92-608
Page Three
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by,
or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
Section 87103.
Accordingly,
a public official may not make, participate in
making,
or attempt to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate family,
or on
any of the official’s economic interests specified in Section
87103 above.
An official’s “immediate family” includes his or her spouse
and dependent children.
(Section 82029.)
The definition of
“immediate family” does not include “son—in—law.”
If the only
relationship between the councilinember and the department head is
that of a “familial” relationship, that fact,
standing alone,
would not require the councilmember to abstain from participating
in the reorganization plan.
“Income” means a payment received,
including but not limited
to any salary,
wage,
advance, dividend, interest, rent,
proceeds
from any sale,
loan,
forgiveness or payment of indebtedness
received by the filer,
reimbursement for expenses, per diem,
or
contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person
other than an employer,
and including any community property
interest in the income of a spouse.
Income also includes an
outstanding loan.
(Section 82030(a).)
Although the exact amount of the rental paid to the
councilmember was not specified,
you stated that the son—in—law
pays fair market rent.
If the amount paid for rent amounts to
$250 or more during the
12 months prior to when the decision will
be rendered, the son—in—law is a source of income to the
councilmember.


Advice File No.
1—92—608
Page Four
The question then is will the decision have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally,
on the source of income.
The effect of a decision is material on any person which has
been a source of income to an official of
$250 or more in the
preceding 12 months if the person is directly involved in a
decision before the official’s agency.
(Regulation
18702.1(a) (1).)
A person is directly involved in a decision before an
official’s agency when that person is a named party in,
or is the
subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency.
(Regulation 18702.1(b) (2).)
In this instance, the city administrator will be presenting a
reorganization plan to the city council for their consideration.
Part of that recommendation would specifically provide for the
assignment of additional duties and an increase in salary to the
position held by the son-in-law.
It does not appear that the
increase in salary is an across—the—board change.
Since the son—
in—law is the subject of the decision before the city council,
this decision appears to have a direct effect on the son-in—law.
4
!
For future reference,
a different standard is used to
determine when the indirect effect of a decision would be
material.
The effect of a decision is material to an individual
who is a source of income if the decision will affect the
individual’s income by $1,000 or more.
Regulation 18702.6(a).
For example,
if the effect of the reorganization plan
increased the salary paid to department heads,
including the son—
in-law,
by $1,000 or more, the decision by the city council would
have a material financial effect on the son—in—law.
The
councilmember would be disqualified from participating in the
reorganization plan.
However,
as stated above,
from the information you provided
in your letter, it appears that the decision will have a direct
effect on the son—in-law in that he is the subject of the
proceeding.
4’
If the decision were only to consider the reorganization plan,
and as a result of that reorganization plan, all department heads
incurred additional duties and one of the effects is that all
department heads received salary increases for absorbing the
additional workload,
then the decision on the reorganization plan
might have an indirect effect on the son—in—law.


Advice File No.
1—92-608
Page Five
Absent any additional information, the city councilmeinber is
prohibited from participating in the decision regarding the
proposed reorganization plan.
If you need further assistance,
or wish to discuss this,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
A
istant General Counsel
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SH/JET/jt

who is also a source of income to the councilmexnber?

material financial effect on the son-in-law.

2,

6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/

(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329 Cc) (3).)
 •

Advice File No.
1—92—608
Page Two
